text:
CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
Restaging Examination — Post-Induction Chemotherapy (Following Completion of 12 Cycles FOLFOX/Bevacizumab)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Marcus T. Delgado, 58-year-old male with known stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with bilobar hepatic metastases, KRAS G12D-mutant, microsatellite stable. Patient has completed 12 cycles of induction FOLFOX plus bevacizumab (cycles 11-12 modified to 5-FU/leucovorin/bevacizumab following oxaliplatin discontinuation for grade 3 cumulative peripheral neuropathy). Prior imaging at 4-cycle mark demonstrated measurable decrease in hepatic target lesions. Current scan obtained to assess response at end of induction regimen and to guide surgical/oncologic decision-making regarding potential conversion hepatectomy. CEA reported at 87 ng/mL, down from 487 ng/mL at baseline. CA 19-9: 64 U/mL.

COMPARISON:
Prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed approximately 8 weeks earlier (4-cycle restaging scan), and staging CT performed at the time of initial diagnosis approximately 6 months prior. MRI liver with hepatobiliary contrast also available for comparison.

TECHNIQUE:
Helical CT acquisition of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis following administration of 100 mL of iohexol (Omnipaque 350) intravenous contrast material via antecubital vein. Images acquired in portal venous phase. Multiplanar reformats (axial, coronal, sagittal) reviewed. Slice thickness 3 mm throughout. No oral contrast administered per institutional protocol. No known contrast allergy. No significant streak artifact.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are well inflated bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules are identified. No new or suspicious parenchymal lesions are seen compared to prior studies. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. No consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinal and hilar contours appear unremarkable. No enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the mediastinal, hilar, or axillary distributions. No pericardial effusion.

Bony Thorax: No lytic, sclerotic, or expansile osseous lesions are identified in the ribs, thoracic vertebral bodies, sternum, or clavicles.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver demonstrates continued response to systemic therapy relative to the baseline and interim studies. There are four previously identified hepatic metastases, all of which show appreciable interval reduction in size:

— Segment VI lesion: This was the dominant hepatic lesion at baseline, measuring 3.8 cm on the staging CT. On the 4-cycle interim scan it had decreased to approximately 2.9 cm. On the current examination, this lesion measures approximately 2.3 cm in greatest axial dimension. It demonstrates peripheral heterogeneous enhancement with relative hypodense center, morphologically consistent with treatment-related necrosis or geographic response. Margins remain slightly irregular but the lesion is less conspicuous than on prior studies. Overall, this lesion has shown substantial downsizing over the course of induction therapy.

— Segment IVa lesion: Previously measured at 2.1 cm on staging CT, 1.6 cm on the 4-cycle scan. On the current study this lesion measures approximately 1.2 cm. It is now hypodense relative to adjacent hepatic parenchyma with no significant arterial or portal phase enhancement. Substantially smaller than on prior studies.

— Segment II lesion: Previously 1.3 cm on staging, 1.0 cm at interim. On the current examination this lesion measures approximately 0.7 cm, now appearing as a small hypoattenuating focus along the left hepatic lobe periphery. Continued reduction in size.

— Segment VII lesion: Previously 0.9 cm on staging, 0.6 cm on 4-cycle scan. On the current study this lesion is now diminutive, measuring approximately 0.4 cm, at the limit of reliable characterization on CT. No new enhancement characteristics to suggest viable tumor.

No new hepatic lesions are identified on this examination. The background hepatic parenchyma is homogeneous without focal steatosis, cirrhosis, or vascular abnormality. Portal and hepatic veins are patent. No biliary ductal dilatation. No perihepatic fluid.

Biliary System: The gallbladder is present with no wall thickening or cholelithiasis. Common bile duct measures approximately 4 mm, within normal limits.

Spleen: Spleen is normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas: Pancreas appears normal in size, contour, and enhancement pattern. No ductal dilatation. No pancreatic lesion identified.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are thin and unremarkable. No adrenal nodule or mass.

Kidneys and Ureters: Bilateral kidneys are normal in size with symmetric cortical enhancement and excretion of contrast material. No hydronephrosis. No renal lesion. No perinephric stranding.

PELVIS AND PRIMARY TUMOR SITE:

Sigmoid Colon: There is a soft tissue mass in the sigmoid colon, consistent with the known primary adenocarcinoma. The lesion appears to have modestly decreased in overall bulk compared to baseline, measuring approximately 3.2 cm in the plane of maximal cross-sectional extent (previously 4.5 cm at baseline, 3.9 cm at interim imaging). There is persistent mild pericolonic fat stranding adjacent to the sigmoid mass; however, this is less prominent than on the staging examination. The luminal narrowing appears similar to the prior study with no features to suggest developing obstruction. The mesorectal/mesocolic fascial planes appear grossly intact in the region.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes: Previously enlarged mesenteric and pericolonic lymph nodes have reduced in conspicuity. The most prominent previously identified node measured 1.4 cm; on the current scan, this region demonstrates several sub-centimeter nodes without definite short-axis lymphadenopathy. No bulky pelvic or inguinal adenopathy.

Bladder and Reproductive Organs: Urinary bladder is decompressed but without obvious wall thickening or intraluminal abnormality. Prostate gland appears within normal size limits. No pelvic free fluid.

Peritoneum and Retroperitoneum: No peritoneal nodularity, omental thickening, or mesenteric infiltration to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites. Retroperitoneal lymph nodes remain within normal size limits. Aorta and major abdominal vessels demonstrate normal caliber without aneurysmal dilatation or significant atherosclerotic disease.

BONES:
Visualized osseous structures including the lumbar spine, sacrum, iliac wings, and proximal femora appear unremarkable. No focal lytic or sclerotic lesions suspicious for osseous metastatic disease.

IMPRESSION:

1. Continued and substantial reduction in size of all four previously identified hepatic metastases following completion of 12 cycles of induction FOLFOX/bevacizumab (cycles 11-12 modified to 5-FU/LV/bevacizumab). The dominant segment VI lesion has decreased from 3.8 cm at baseline to 2.3 cm on the current study; the segment IVa, II, and VII lesions have all diminished proportionally, with the smallest (segment VII) now nearly imperceptible. Overall hepatic tumor burden is markedly reduced compared to baseline. The morphologic appearance of the segment VI lesion is consistent with partial treatment response with probable central necrosis.

2. Modest decrease in size of the primary sigmoid colon tumor, now measuring approximately 3.2 cm compared to 4.5 cm at staging. Pericolonic fat stranding is less extensive. No imaging features of obstruction or perforation.

3. Previously noted regional lymphadenopathy has substantially decreased in conspicuity, with no lymph nodes currently meeting size criteria for enlargement.

4. No pulmonary nodules identified. No new sites of disease. No peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites. No bone lesions.

5. Despite the favorable tumor response observed throughout the hepatic parenchyma, the metastatic disease remains bilobar in distribution (segments II, IVa, VI, and VII all involved, though substantially smaller). The adequacy of future liver remnant following potential hepatic resection will require careful hepatobiliary surgical assessment; this imaging alone cannot predict resectability without volumetric analysis and multidisciplinary input.

Overall, findings are consistent with a meaningful and ongoing radiographic response to induction chemotherapy. No evidence of new distant metastatic disease at this time.
